name: Wu Song
description: Wu Song, a prominent figure in Chinese folklore and one of the 108 Stars of Destiny in the classic novel *Water Margin*, is revered as a heroic outlaw and a symbol of courage, justice, and righteous rebellion against oppressive forces. He is often depicted as a skilled martial artist, possessing immense strength and unwavering determination. Wu Song's legendary exploits include his famous slaying of a tiger with his bare hands on Jingyang Ridge, an act that cemented his reputation as a formidable warrior. His story is intertwined with themes of loyalty, brotherhood, and the fight against corruption within the Song Dynasty. Wu Song embodies the spirit of the common people rising against injustice and tyranny, making him a powerful and enduring symbol in Chinese culture. He is worshipped as a deity in some traditions, particularly among those who seek protection and courage in the face of adversity. His narrative serves as a potent reminder of the importance of integrity and standing up for what is right, even in the face of overwhelming odds.
subtitle: Tiger Slayer

keyMyths.title: Wu Song Fights the Tiger
keyMyths.description: While traveling through Jingyang Ridge, Wu Song ignored warnings about a man-eating tiger. After drinking heavily at a tavern, he encountered the ferocious beast. Despite his drunken state, Wu Song bravely fought the tiger with his bare hands, eventually subduing and killing it. This act made him a local hero and earned him a position as a constable.

keyMyths.title: Wu Song Kills Pan Jinlian and Ximen Qing
keyMyths.description: After discovering that his brother, Wu Dalang, was poisoned by his adulterous wife, Pan Jinlian, and her lover, Ximen Qing, Wu Song sought revenge. He meticulously gathered evidence and, in a dramatic confrontation, killed both Pan Jinlian and Ximen Qing. While his actions were considered murder, many viewed them as justified retribution for their crimes.

keyMyths.title: Wu Song Becomes a Monk
keyMyths.description: Following his act of vengeance, Wu Song was exiled. During his exile, he sought refuge in a monastery and became a monk. However, his rebellious nature and inability to adhere to monastic rules led him to eventually leave the monastery and join the Liangshan outlaws. This transition marks his complete embrace of the outlaw life.

sources.author: Shi Nai'an

sources.text: An English translation and adaptation of *Water Margin*, providing a Western audience with access to the Wu Song narrative.

associations.name: Staff
associations.significance: Represents his martial prowess and ability to overcome opponents with skill rather than brute force alone.

associations.name: Tiger
associations.significance: Symbolizes his immense courage and strength, particularly evident in his famous tiger-slaying feat.

associations.name: Justice
associations.significance: Represents his unwavering commitment to upholding righteousness and fighting against corruption and oppression.

associations.name: Outlaw
associations.significance: Highlights his rebellion against unjust authority and his willingness to operate outside the bounds of the law to achieve justice.

associations.name: Loyalty
associations.significance: Demonstrates his dedication to his friends and allies, a key characteristic of the heroes in Water Margin.

associations.name: Alcohol
associations.significance: Often associated with his moments of bravery and recklessness, as he frequently gains courage from drinking.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Both are renowned for their incredible strength and legendary feats, such as slaying formidable beasts.
Similar to Wu Song, Beowulf is celebrated for his courage in battling monstrous creatures and defending his people.
Both are legendary warriors known for their exceptional martial skills, fierce determination, and unwavering loyalty.

worshipPractices.name: Folk Temples
worshipPractices.description: In some regions, small shrines or altars may be dedicated to Wu Song, where people offer incense and prayers for protection, courage, and justice. These are often localized and informal.

cultural.modernLegacy: Wu Song remains a popular figure in Chinese literature, film, television, and video games, embodying the spirit of the righteous hero fighting against corruption and injustice. His stories continue to inspire and entertain audiences, solidifying his status as a cultural icon.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: Wu Song's early life is marked by hardship and a strong sense of justice. Growing up in a rural village, he developed exceptional martial arts skills and a reputation for bravery. His story begins with an act of vengeance, as he seeks to avenge his brother's death. This sets him on a path that leads him to become a fugitive and, eventually, a hero among the outlaws of Liangshan. Wu Song's formative experiences instilled in him a deep-seated sense of right and wrong, fueling his later acts of defiance against corrupt officials and oppressive forces. Even before his most famous exploits, his character was defined by a willingness to stand up for what he believed in, regardless of the personal cost. This unwavering moral compass is a key element of his enduring appeal.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: Wu Song is best known for two legendary feats: slaying a man-eating tiger on Jingyang Ridge and his involvement in the bloody events at the Mandarin Duck Tower. The tiger-slaying incident cemented his reputation as a fearless hero, showcasing his immense strength and courage. His actions at the Mandarin Duck Tower, while morally complex, demonstrate his unwavering loyalty and his willingness to take extreme measures to avenge injustice. These events propelled him into a life of outlawry, where he joined the Liangshan rebels and became one of the 108 Stars of Destiny. Wu Song's quests are not simply about physical prowess; they are about moral choices and the struggle against corruption. He embodies the spirit of rebellion against oppressive forces, making him a symbol of resistance and justice.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: Wu Song's legacy extends far beyond the pages of *Water Margin*. He has become a cultural icon in China, representing courage, justice, and the fight against oppression. His stories have been adapted into countless films, television shows, and operas, ensuring that his heroic deeds continue to inspire new generations. Wu Song's image is often invoked to represent the spirit of resistance and the importance of standing up for what is right, even in the face of overwhelming odds. His enduring popularity speaks to the universal appeal of his character and the timeless themes of justice, loyalty, and courage that he embodies. He remains a powerful symbol of the common person's struggle against corruption and tyranny, solidifying his place as a legendary hero in Chinese culture.

shortDescription: A hero of Water Margin, Wu Song is famed for slaying a tiger and fighting injustice. He embodies courage and rebellion against oppression.
